critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that provides an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It highlights features such as pan and zoom, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git integration, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon offers an infinite 2D canvas for terminal sessions, allowing users to place, resize, and group terminals freely.
It supports multiple workspaces with color-coding and various layout modes for organizing panels.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color, mouse reporting, and other advanced features.
Integration with AI agents like Claude Code and Codex is included, with session persistence and usage tracking.
Built-in git status panel provides inline diffs and file change tracking without context switching.
Interactive features include clickable URLs and file paths, a live settings editor with instant application of changes, and session persistence across restarts.
The project is open source, written primarily in Rust, and available for Linux, macOS, and Windows platforms.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made about Horizon align with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which details its features, architecture, and usage. The description of the terminal emulation powered by Alacritty and the integration of AI agents and git functionality are consistent with the repository documentation. The project is actively maintained and open source, supporting the accuracy of the technical claims.
considerations: The article does not mention any benchmarks or performance comparisons with other terminal emulators to substantiate claims about speed or efficiency.
User experience aspects such as stability, resource usage, and compatibility with various systems are not discussed.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and interactive terminal features are not addressed.
The learning curve or usability for new users transitioning from traditional terminal emulators is not covered.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal, with users sharing their experiences, feature requests, and comparisons to similar tools. Comments highlight usability concerns, potential improvements like zooming and multi-screen support, and references to related projects.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback with constructive suggestions and accurate observations about dependencies and documentation.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: Offers practical UI/UX improvement ideas grounded in real use cases, enhancing the infinite canvas concept.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and user perspective that helps frame Horizon's niche and potential user base.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
Spatial memory is really underutilised in computing.

When libghostty[0] releases maybe you could use that so you don't have to build everything from scratch.

[0]: https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant suggestion to leverage existing projects, which could improve development efficiency.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
Feels very Niri like, in a positive way. Niri's "scrollable" interface always has room to the right & down!

One of the Niri tools I built for myself is to let me insert a new row up top, to expand up. Thats afaik not super easy in base Niri, constraints the spatial growth some.

I'm pretty close to having some persistence of terminal windows, tmux, etc in Niri, with some tools to dump current state nicely. Doing an actual restore operation would be a good thing to build towards.
comments.analysis: Shares insights from a similar tool and suggests useful features that could enhance Horizon's functionality.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal expectation that is unrelated to the actual project scope, adding little constructive value.

comments.author: sakopov
comments.text: Will there be any built releases available?
comments.analysis: A straightforward inquiry about release availability, relevant but minimal in content.

comments.author: hokkos
comments.text: The last thing I want is the figma experience for my terminal
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective dislike for a complex UI approach, but lacks detailed reasoning or constructive feedback.
